column3: Find a combination x₁w₁ + x₂w₂ + x₃w₃ that gives the zero vector where w₁=[1,2,3], w₂=[4,5,6], w₃=[7,8,9]. Are these vectors independent or dependent?
column4: w₁ − 2w₂ + w₃ = 0. The vectors are dependent and lie in a plane.
column8: Models may state "dependent" without showing the actual combination

column3:
(a) Find a 3 by 3 permutation matrix with P 3 = I (but not P = I). 
(b) Find a 4 by 4 permutation Pwith P4 = I.
column4:
Correct Answer: P = [[0,0,1],[1,0,0],[0,1,0]], cycling rows so P³ = I. (b) Let P be the block diagonal matrix with 1 and P on the diagonal: P = ( 
0 P 
). Since 
P 3 = I, also P3 = I. So P4 = P<>I.
column8: Models may give P² = I instead — wrong cycle length; must verify P³ = I explicitly

column3:
Problem 18: True or false (check addition or give a counterexample): 
(a) The symmetric matrices in M (with AT = A) form a subspace. 
(b) The skew-symmetric matrices in M (with AT = −A) form a subspace. 
(c) The unsymmetric matrices in M (with AT<>A) form a subspace.
column8: Models may answer correctly but skip justification for scalar multiplication

column3:
Suppose A and B are n by n matrices, and AB = I. Prove from rank(AB) ≤
rank(A) that the rank of A is n. So A is invertible and B must be its two-sided inverse (Section 2.5). 
Therefore BA = I (which is not so obvious!).
column4: n = rank(I) = rank(AB) ≤ rank(A) ≤ n, so rank(A) = n and A is invertible.
column8: Models often prove rank = n but forget to conclude invertibility

column3: Start with u₀=(1,0). Multiply repeatedly by Markov matrix A=[[0.8,0.3],[0.2,0.7]]. Find u₁, u₂, u₃ and state what property all four vectors share.
column4: u₁=[0.8,0.2], u₂=[0.7,0.3], u₃=[0.65,0.35]. All vectors have components that sum to one.

column3:
If P 2 = P show that (I − P )2 = I − P . When P projects onto the 
column space of A, I − P projects onto the .
column4: (I−P)² = I−2P+P² = I−P. I−P projects onto the left nullspace of A.

column3:
If the entries in every row of A add to zero, solve 
Ax = 0 to prove det A = 0. If those entries add to one, show that det(A − I) = 0. 
Does this mean det A = I?
column4: Rows summing to zero means x=(1,1,...,1) solves Ax=0 so det A=0. Rows of A−I sum to zero so det(A−I)=0.

column3: Suppose you solve Ax=b for three special right sides: Ax₁=[1,0,0], Ax₂=[0,1,0], Ax₃=[0,0,1]. If x₁, x₂, x₃ are columns of matrix X, what is AX?
column4: AX = I. X is the inverse of A.

column3:
Problem Set 1 Section 2.5, Problem 7
If A has row 1 + row 2 = row 3, show that A is not invertible by: (a) explaining why Ax=(1,0,0) cannot have a solution, (b) finding which right sides (b₁,b₂,b₃) might allow a solution, (c) stating what happens to row 3 during elimination.
column4: (a) A₁·x+A₂·x=A₃·x means 1+0=0 — contradiction. (b) b₁+b₂=b₃ must hold. (c) Row 3 becomes zero during elimination.

column3: Three planes can fail to have an intersection point even if no planes are parallel. Find a third equation that cannot be solved together with x+y+z=0 and x−2y−z=1
column4: Row 3 must be a linear combination of rows 1 and 2 but right-hand side must violate that relation. Example: 2x+5y+4z=1.
column7: Systems of Equations and Singular Systems
column8: Many valid answers exist

column3: Compute L and U for A=[[a,a,a,a],[a,b,b,b],[a,b,c,c],[a,b,c,d]]. Find four conditions on a,b,c,d for four pivots.
column4: Pivots are a, b−a, c−b, d−c. Conditions: a≠0, b≠a, c≠b, d≠c.
column8: Models frequently compute L correctly but list wrong pivot conditions

column3: How is the nullspace N(C) related to N(A) and N(B) if C=[A;B]?
column4: N(C) = N(A) ∩ N(B). Cx=0 if and only if Ax=0 and Bx=0 simultaneously.
column8: Models may say N(C) = N(A) + N(B); this is a classic sign error; correct answer is intersection not sum

column3: A is the 4×4 identity with last column removed (4×3). Project b=(1,2,3,4) onto column space of A. Find projection matrix P and the projection.
column8: Models may attempt P=A(AᵀA)⁻¹Aᵀ formula correctly but make arithmetic errors in 4×4 setting

column3: Write E=‖Ax−b‖² as a sum of four squares. Find ∂E/∂C=0 and ∂E/∂D=0 and divide by 2 to get normal equations AᵀAx̂=Aᵀb for the least squares line through points (0,0),(1,8),(3,8),(4,20).
column4: Normal equations: 4C+8D=36 and 8C+26D=112.
column8: Multi-step problem ;models often set up E correctly but make errors taking partial derivatives or forget factor of 2

column3:
Problem Set  8 Problem 6. (§6.4,#7) 
1 b (a) Find a symmetric matrix that has a negative eigenvalue. b 1 
(b) How do you know it must have a negative pivot? 
(c) How do you know it can’t have two negative eigenvalues?
column4:
Take b>1. Eigenvalues are 1±b so one is negative. det=1−b² <0 so one pivot must be negative. c) The product of the eigenvalues equals the determinant, which is negative in this case. Two negative 
numbers cannothave a negativeproduct
column8: Models may correctly find b>1 but fail to connect determinant sign to pivot sign

column3:
Problem Set 9 Section 6.5, Problem 26
Find the Cholesky factorization C (upper triangular) for A=[[1,1,1],[1,2,2],[1,2,7]]
column8: Models often forget to take square root of last pivot

column3:
Problem Set 3  Section 3.3, Problem 25
Every m×n matrix of rank r reduces to (m×r)(r×n). Write the 3×4 matrix A = [[1,1,2,4],[1,2,2,5],[1,3,2,6]] as the product of pivot columns times first r rows of R
column4: A = [[1,1],[1,2],[1,3]] × [[1,0,2,3],[0,1,0,1]]. Pivot columns of A multiplied by first 2 rows of R.
column8: Models frequently identify pivot columns correctly but make errors writing the R rows

column3:
Problem Set 5  Section 4.1, Problem 9
If AᵀAx=0 then Ax=0. Reason: Ax is in the nullspace of Aᵀ and also in the column space of A and those spaces are orthogonal. Conclusion: AᵀA has the same nullspace as A.
Complete the reasoning and fill in the blanks to prove this key fact.
column4: Ax is in the nullspace of Aᵀ AND in the column space of A. Those spaces are orthogonal. Therefore Ax=0. So AᵀA has the same nullspace as A.
column8: Fill-in reasoning problem

column3:
Problem Set 6 Section 5.1, Problem 18
Use row operations to show that the 3×3 Vandermonde determinant is:
det([[1,a,a²],[1,b,b²],[1,c,c²]]) = (b−a)(c−a)(c−b)
column4: Subtract row 1 from rows 2 and 3, then factor (b−a) from row 2 and (c−a) from row 3. After one more elimination step: det = (b−a)(c−a)(c−b).
column8: Multi-step elimination required

column3:
Problem Set 2  Section 2.6, Problem 18
If A=LDU and also A=L₁D₁U₁, derive L₁⁻¹LD=D₁U₁U⁻¹ and use it to prove uniqueness: L=L₁, D=D₁, U=U₁.
column4: Left side is lower triangular, right side upper triangular, so both are diagonal. Since both have diagonal 1s they equal I, giving L=L₁, U=U₁, D=D₁.
column8: Multi-step proof

column3:
Problem Set 3 Section 3.4, Problem 25
Write down all relations between r, m, n if Ax=b has: (a) no solution for some b, (b) infinitely many solutions for every b, (c) exactly one solution for some b no solution for other b, (d) exactly one solution for every b.
column4:
(a) The system has less than full row rank: r<m. 
(b) The system has full row rank, and less than full column rank: m = r<n. 
(c) The system has full column rank, and less than full row rank: n = r<m. 
(d) The systemhasfull row and column rank(i.e., is invertible): n = r = m.
column8: Abstract reasoning problem

column3:
Problem Set 6  Section 4.4, Problem 18
Find orthonormal vectors A, B, C by Gram-Schmidt from a=(1,−1,0,0), b=(0,1,−1,0), c=(0,0,1,−1). Show {A,B,C} is a basis for vectors perpendicular to d=(1,1,1,1).
column4: A=(1/√2)(1,−1,0,0), B=(1/√6)(1,1,−2,0), C=(1/2√3)(1,1,1,−3). All three are perpendicular to d and span the 3-dimensional subspace.
column8: Most complex calculation in medium-hard range

column3:
Problem Set 8  Section 6.3, Problem 24
Write A=[[1,3],[0,0]] as SΛS⁻¹. Find matrix exponential eᴬᵗ by multiplying SeΛᵗS⁻¹. Verify eᴬᵗ and its derivative at t=0
column4: Λ=[[1,0],[0,3]], S=[[-1/2,1/2],[0,1]]. eᴬᵗ=[[eᵗ, (3e³ᵗ−eᵗ)/2],[0,e³ᵗ]]. At t=0 gives I; derivative at t=0 gives A.
column8: Two verification steps required

column3:
Problem Set 10 Section 6.6, Problem 12
Compare JM with MK for Jordan matrices J and K. If JM=MK show M is not invertible, proving J is not similar to K
column4: Setting JM=MK forces specific entries of M to zero making the second row or fourth row dependent, so M is always singular and J cannot be similar to K.
column8: Highest hallucination risk problem

column3:
Problem Set 10  Section 6.7, Problem 4
Find eigenvalues and unit eigenvectors of AᵀA and AAᵀ for Fibonacci matrix A=[[1,1],[1,0]]. Construct the full SVD and verify A=UΣVᵀ.
column4: Eigenvalues (3±√5)/2. Singular values σ=(1±√5)/2. Full SVD constructed from these eigenvectors and verified.
column8: Most computationally demanding problem

column3:
Problem Set 9  Section 6.5, Problem 33
When A and B are symmetric positive definite, AB might not be symmetric. Prove its eigenvalues are still positive by starting from ABx=λx and taking dot products with Bx.
column4: ABx)ᵀBx=(Bx)ᵀA(Bx)=λxᵀBx. Since B positive definite xᵀBx>0 and A positive definite means (Bx)ᵀA(Bx)>0, so λ>0.
column8: Abstract proof

column3:
Problem Set 10 Section 6.6, Problem 22
If an n×n matrix A has all eigenvalues λ=0, prove that Aⁿ is the zero matrix.
column4: Each Jordan block Jᵢ of size nᵢ satisfies Jᵢⁿⁱ=0 since there is no diagonal nᵢ diagonals above the main diagonal. Since all blocks satisfy Jᵢⁿ=0 and Aⁿ is similar to a block diagonal matrix with these blocks, Aⁿ=0. Alternatively by Cayley-Hamilton: all eigenvalues zero means characteristic polynomial is xⁿ so Aⁿ=0.
column8: Two valid proof methods; Jordan block argument or Cayley-Hamilton
